response.description_long:
Serverless GPU inference traditionally forces a trade-off: keep expensive GPUs warm around the clock or tolerate cold starts that can take a minute or more while models initialize. Modelgrid eliminates that choice with a snapshot architecture that restores fully initialized models from a distributed cache in under 2 seconds, even for models tens of gigabytes in size.

The product is an autoscaling API endpoint for any model running on PyTorch or vLLM. Users deploy a model via CLI or a Hugging Face model ID, and Modelgrid handles batching, queueing, scale-from-zero autoscaling, and GPU selection across A100 and H100 classes. Its key technical innovation is a snapshot that captures not just weights but the full process memory, CUDA context, and allocator state. This snapshot lives on NVMe within the same rack as the GPUs, enabling deterministic restores that take 2 seconds rather than minutes. Scale-out is uniform—the tenth replica comes up as fast as the first—and new model versions are built and validated against the snapshot before traffic shifts, preventing bad deploys from affecting running endpoints.

Target users are teams shipping AI features with spiky or unpredictable traffic—overnight batch jobs, consumer apps with regional peaks, or agents that fan out to many models. The platform supports language, embedding, diffusion, and speech-to-text models, with continuous batching and paged attention configured for vLLM workloads. For custom PyTorch, the Python SDK wraps a predict function and infers batching behavior from the function signature. Streaming responses, server-sent events, and long-running jobs up to 30 minutes are supported on every endpoint.

Pricing is per-second, metered on compute time only; queue time and snapshot storage for active endpoints are free. A100 40 GB starts at $2.10 per hour equivalent, A100 80 GB at $3.10, and H100 at $5.60, all billed by the second with no minimums or reservations required. A documented example shows an embedding endpoint on an A100 40 GB serving 10 minutes of traffic per day meters roughly 6 GPU-hours a month—versus 720 hours for an always-warm alternative.

Modelgrid runs its own capacity in three US regions, each with its own snapshot cache. Endpoints deploy across at least two regions with health-based routing and automatic failover. The company publishes live and historical latency and restore-time figures, as well as uptime history, on a public status page. It is SOC 2 Type II certified, runs workloads in isolated VMs with encrypted snapshots and per-tenant keys, and supports IP allowlists and private networking.

Founded in San Francisco in 2023 by infrastructure engineers Ilya Sorokin and Grace Tan, Modelgrid was born from the observation that cold starts are not a physics problem but a consequence of replaying initialization that could have been saved. By owning the serving stack from the rack to the scheduler, they made the snapshot restore path fast enough to change the economics of serverless GPU inference.
